metadata.title: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. TEAL = superposition / sloshing / dynamic; CRIMSON = eigenstate / still / frozen

beats.narration_text: Freeze two energy eigenstates — each one static, neither one moving. Now add them. The sum sloshes back and forth like a pendulum. Two stillnesses, combined, become motion.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: what is a quantum superposition, really?

beats.narration_text: Each energy state in a box sits perfectly still. Add two of them together and suddenly the probability sloshes wall to wall — a hundred times faster than a heartbeat.

card.copy: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: ground state (n=1) single hump and first excited state (n=2) two humps in a particle-in-a-box, side by side, each labeled as stationary with a still probability cloud"

beats.narration_text: The ground state: a single hump in the center. The first excited state: two humps, one on each side. Each one is a stationary state — its probability density never moves.
Both states are frozen in time. Mix them and you get a completely new behavior — the probability swings back and forth. How does adding two stationary things produce something that moves?

card.copy: Two stationary states. Each one frozen. Add them together: the probability sloshes wall to wall. How can two still things make something that moves?

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two clock hands in the complex plane rotating at different rates omega1 and omega2 — label each with its energy eigenstate and rotation frequency"

beats.narration_text: Each eigenstate rotates in the complex plane at its own rate — set by its energy. State one rotates at omega-one, state two at omega-two.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two clock hands drifting apart and back together over time — show the angle between them oscillating and label the beat frequency as omega2-omega1"

beats.narration_text: When you add the two, the combined probability density depends on the angle between the two clocks. As time passes, the two hands drift apart — and then come back together — at the beat frequency.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a probability cloud in a box sloshing left and right as the two clocks go in and out of phase — annotate with 'in phase: left' and 'out of phase: right'"

beats.narration_text: When the two clocks are in phase, the left side of the box gets the probability. When they are out of phase, the right side does. The cloud sloshes at the beat frequency — E-two minus E-one, divided by h-bar.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: energy level diagram for a superposition — show the two eigenstate energies E1 and E2 as horizontal lines, with the superposition oscillating between them, and label 'total energy is not definite'"

beats.narration_text: The energy of the superposition doesn't change — it is still a mix of the two eigenstate energies. The sloshing is about position, not energy. The total energy is definite only if you pick one eigenstate; in a mix, it is not.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two pure audio tones close in frequency combining to produce a slowly pulsing beat envelope — label tone frequencies and the beat frequency as their difference"

beats.narration_text: This is the quantum analog of a beat note in sound: two pure tones close in frequency combine to make a slowly pulsing envelope. The Bohr frequency plays the role of the beat frequency.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: hydrogen atom n=1 to n=2 Bohr frequency calculation — show 2.5e15 Hz beat frequency compared to UV light frequency on a logarithmic frequency axis"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: for the hydrogen atom in a superposition of n-equals-1 and n-equals-2, the Bohr frequency is about 2.5 times ten to the 15 hertz — the electron oscillates between those states about as fast as ultraviolet light oscillates.
Each eigenstate is frozen. Their phases rotate at different rates. The difference in rates is the beat — and the beat is what makes the probability slosh.

card.copy: Each eigenstate frozen. Phases drift apart. The difference in rates is the beat. The beat makes the probability slosh.

beats.narration_text: The sloshing is happening right now in every superposition. Paste this into Claude and make it visible.

props.segment: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One
props.command: claude "simulate a quantum superposition of the n=1 and n=2 states in a particle-in-a-box — animate the probability density sloshing over time"

beats.narration_text: If this made superposition feel real, subscribe. Medhavy.

props.title: Why Two Frozen States Make a Sloshing One.
